mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the entire history of medicine from ancient through modern times, the central topic of section A.
Epidemiology is a core medical discipline related to public health (B.2.d) and the study of disease transmission (A.2.e).
Pandemics relate to bacteriology (A.2.c), immunology (A.3.b), and tropical medicine (A.3.d.iii).
Plague illustrates the evolution of medicine from ancient practices through the establishment of bacteriology.
Sections A.2.c (bacteriology), A.3.a (antibiotics), and A.3.b (immunology) address the medical response to infectious disease.
HIV/AIDS is a major 20th-century medical challenge, relevant to immunology (A.3.b) and tropical medicine (A.3.d.iii).
Section A.3.b.ii covers vaccines for viral diseases including yellow fever, influenza, poliomyelitis, and measles.
Section A.2.c covers the establishment of bacteriology, germ theory verification, and disease-producing organisms.
Section A.3.b covers advances in immunology including vaccines for bacterial and viral diseases.
Section A.3.d.ii specifically covers cancer research and treatment of abnormal cell growths.
Section A.3.a covers chemotherapy including antibiotics and synthetic drugs, and B.3 covers pharmacology.
Section A.2.d covers the discovery and use of anesthesia, and A.4.e covers inhalation anesthetic procedures.
Anatomical discoveries are discussed throughout the history from ancient times (A.1.b-c) through Renaissance dissection (A.1.f.i).
Section A.2.b covers advances in physiology, and B.3 lists medical physiology as a nonclinical specialty.
Section A.3.c covers endocrinology including insulin, cortisone, and sex hormones, and B.2.e lists endocrinology.
Section A.3.d.i covers nutrition and treatment of deficiency diseases through vitamins, and B.3 lists nutrition.
Section B.2.d lists public health as a clinical specialty.
Section B.1.g lists psychiatry as a hospital residency specialty.
Clinical psychology connects to psychiatry (B.1.g) and the assessment and treatment of mental disorders.
Psychotherapy connects to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a therapeutic approach within medical practice.
CBT is a leading therapeutic modality used in psychiatry (B.1.g).
Schizophrenia is a major psychiatric condition relevant to psychiatry (B.1.g).
Depression relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a major area of medical practice.
Anxiety disorders are a major concern of psychiatry (B.1.g).
Autism relates to neurology (B.1.f) and psychiatry (B.1.g) as neurodevelopmental conditions.
Addiction medicine relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) and pharmacology (B.3).
Psychopathy relates to psychiatry (B.1.g) as a clinical condition.
Dementia relates to neurology (B.1.f) and psychiatry (B.1.g).
Dyslexia relates to neurology (B.1.f) and understanding of neurodevelopmental conditions.
Neurology (B.1.f) is a hospital residency specialty, and the brain is central to neuroscience in medicine.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationaleAI: Sleep medicine relates to neurology (B.1.f) and psychiatry (B.1.g).
Pain management connects to anesthesiology (B.1.h) and the historical development of anesthesia (A.2.d).
Section B.3 lists gerontology as a nonclinical specialty and basic medical science.
Section A.2.a mentions the cell and cellular pathology as foundational 19th-century medical concepts.
Section A.4.d covers blood transfusions and intravenous techniques in 20th-century surgery.
Section C.1 covers the history and practice of dentistry as a discipline affiliated with medicine.
Medical ethics relates to the Hippocratic charter of medical conduct (A.1.d.ii) and ethical dimensions of practice.
Section B.2.b covers medical jurisprudence as a clinical specialty.
Forensic science connects to medical jurisprudence (B.2.b), pathology (B.1.h), and toxicology (B.2.g).
Veterinary science is closely affiliated with medicine, sharing foundational sciences and methods.
Section A.1.g.i highlights the importance of the microscope to medical studies.
Section A.2.e mentions X-ray discovery, and B.1.a lists radiology as a hospital specialty.
Circadian rhythms are relevant to medical physiology (B.3) and understanding of sleep, hormones, and metabolism.
Molecular understanding underpins pharmacology (B.3) and chemotherapy (A.3.a) at the molecular level.
